content:
Connections went out. They never came back. PgBouncer couldn’t save us from ourselves.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Four thousand two hundred connections to a database whose max_connections is 500. The app didn't crash. It queued until everything did.
Saturday 9:00 AM. API latency p99 12s. Error rate 18%. FATAL: sorry, too many clients already in logs — 4,700 occurrences in 20 minutes.
PgBouncer in transaction mode. Pool size 500. App servers: 40 pods.
Each pod configured: DB_POOL_SIZE=100. Math: 4,000 potential connections. Postgres: 500 max. PgBouncer: crying.
Root cause: connection leak in deploy Friday 11 PM — asyncpg pool not closed on shutdown hook. Every pod restart leaked ~12 connections. Rolling deploy 40 pods. Friday night leak accumulated. Saturday traffic opened the wound.
The leak that survived restarts
# broken lifespan — Friday deploy
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app):
pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(dsn, min_size=10, max_size=100)
app.state.db = pool
yield
# missing: await pool.close()Pods restarted. Old connections on Postgres: idle in transaction or idle — never released.
SELECT state, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE datname = 'api_prod'
GROUP BY state;
-- idle in transaction | 2847
-- idle | 1203
-- active | 89
-- total ~4139 against max_connections=500PgBouncer queued new requests. Queue grew. Timeouts cascaded.
PgBouncer vs application leak
PgBouncer saves you from connection count per request. It does not save you from connections never returned.
Transaction mode: connection returned after COMMIT. Leaked server-side backend: still held.
# pgbouncer pools at incident peak
psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c "SHOW POOLS;"
# api_prod | active | 500 | waiting | 1847
# all 500 server connections busy or dead, 1847 clients waiting500 server connections to Postgres — all consumed by leaked backends from dead pods.
→ PostgreSQL War Room — connection exhaustion diagnosis tree. pg_stat_activity, PgBouncer SHOW POOLS, emergency terminate patterns.
Five-minute first response
-- 1. Connection count by state
SELECT state, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC;-- 2. Oldest idle-in-transaction
SELECT pid, state, xact_start, state_change, left(query, 80)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'
ORDER BY xact_start LIMIT 20;-- 3. By application name
SELECT application_name, count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity
GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC;-- 4. Kill stale idle-in-transaction > 10 min (emergency)
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'
AND state_change < now() - interval '10 minutes';9:14 AM — terminated 2,400 stale backends. Breathing room 90 seconds.
9:16 AM — leak still active in running pods. Terminated again by 9:22.
9:24 AM — hotfix deploy: await pool.close() in lifespan. Rolling restart.
9:38 AM — connections stable 180. Errors 0.4%.
→ Your Database Is Bleeding Money. The Incident Playbook. — connection pool management, PgBouncer config, real incident patterns. The Friday missing pool.close() is case-file material.
The math we didn’t do before Friday
pods (40) × pool_size (100) = 4000 potential
max_connections (500) = hard ceiling
ratio: 8× overcommitRule: pods × pool_size ≤ 0.8 × max_connections (reserve 20% for admin, migrations, monitoring).
With 500 max: 4,000 budget at pod level → 100 total across 40 pods → 2–3 per pod with PgBouncer, or 12 per pod max without overcommit.
Friday config: 100 per pod. Fantasy.
# fixed deployment
env:
- name: DB_POOL_SIZE
value: "10" # 40 × 10 = 400, under 500 with headroomidle_in_transaction_session_timeout
Postgres kills connections idle in transaction too long. We didn’t have it.
ALTER DATABASE api_prod SET idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = '60s';Would have killed Friday leaks within 60 seconds of pod death orphaning them. Saturday 9 AM would have been quieter.
Tradeoff: legitimate long transactions die. We have none > 60s by design. Verified in pg_stat_statements.
Connection leak detection before Saturday
Metrics we added:
→ pg_stat_activity count by state — alert > 400 → PgBouncer waiting clients — alert > 50 → App metric: db_pool_checked_out vs db_pool_size per pod → Deploy annotation + connection count overlay in Grafana
Friday 11 PM deploy — connection count stair-stepped with each pod restart. Visible in Grafana. No alert threshold.
# now in deploy watch dashboard
# connections vs pod restarts — divergence = leakPgBouncer transaction mode settings
[databases]
api_prod = host=postgres.internal dbname=api_prod[pgbouncer]
pool_mode = transaction
max_client_conn = 2000
default_pool_size = 100
reserve_pool_size = 10
server_idle_timeout = 30server_idle_timeout = 30 — drop dead server connections faster. Helps cleanup, doesn't fix app leak.
Real fix: close pool on shutdown. Enforce pool size math. idle_in_transaction_session_timeout.
Saturday customer cost
38 minutes degraded API. Checkout included. Estimated $2,100 lost revenue.
Hotfix: one line + pool size config. 4 hours engineer time Saturday.
Prevention: lint rule in CI — lifespan must call pool.close(). $0 if you catch it pre-merge.
Cheaper than 4,200 connections arguing with 500 slots.
What we enforce now
→ pods × DB_POOL_SIZE ≤ 0.8 × max_connections — CI check on deployment manifests → idle_in_transaction_session_timeout = 60s on all prod databases → Lifespan template in service generator — pool.close() cannot be deleted without noqa + review → Grafana alert: connection count increase > 20% within 10 min of deploy
Friday’s missing await pool.close() was one line. Saturday's 4,200 connections were the invoice.
Rolling deploy + leak = compound interest
Friday 11 PM deploy: 40 pods, 3 waves.
Each wave: old pod SIGTERM, pool not closed, ~12 backends orphaned per pod.
Wave math: 40 × 12 = 480 leaked backends minimum. Overnight idle traffic held them open. Saturday morning traffic exhausted PgBouncer queue.
Lesson: connection leaks scale with deploy frequency. One bad deploy + weekly rollouts = slow bleed. One bad deploy + Friday night full rollout = Saturday fire.
preStop hook — second line of defense
lifecycle:
preStop:
exec:
command: ["python", "-c", "import asyncio; asyncio.run(app.state.db.close())"]Belt and suspenders after lifespan fix. preStop runs before SIGTERM delivery. Catches pools if developer forgets lifespan.
Not a substitute for correct lifespan. Backup for human error.
max_connections budgeting — the spreadsheet
We built a simple budget doc:
Postgres max_connections: 500
Reserve (admin, monitoring): -50
Available for apps: 450PgBouncer default_pool_size: 100
App pods: 40 × pool 10: 400
Headroom: 50Any deploy changing pod count or pool size must update spreadsheet. CI checks manifest against budget.
Friday: 40 × 100 = 4000 would have failed CI. Would have failed code review if CI existed.
Connection storm after hotfix deploy
9:24 AM hotfix rolling restart: brief connection spike as new pods opened pools before old pods released.
Without idle_in_transaction_session_timeout: second wave of orphans.
With timeout: stale backends died within 60s. Spike visible, not catastrophic.
9:38 AM stable 180 connections. Peak during restart 420 — under 500 limit for first time since 9:00.
Small config change. Difference between restart recovery in 90 seconds vs second incident.
The CI lint that would have caught Friday
# ruff custom rule — lifespan must close pool
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app):
pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(...)
try:
yield
finally:
await pool.close() # requiredFriday PR missing finally block. Merged 11 PM. Lint rule shipped Monday 2 PM. Too late for Saturday. In time for never again.
Pool size CI check on same PR:
# deployment check
pods: 40
DB_POOL_SIZE: 100
max_connections: 500
# FAIL: 40*100 > 0.8*500Two lines of prevention. 4,200 connections of cure.
Saturday postmortem — the graph that convinced leadership
Grafana panel: connection count vs pod restart events. Staircase pattern obvious.
Each deploy wave: step up. Never step down until manual terminate.
Leadership understood leak in one screenshot. Prior explanations about asyncpg pools bounced off. Graph didn't.
Budget approved for connection monitoring SaaS we should have built ourselves. We built it ourselves anyway. Cheaper.
PgBouncer SHOW STATS — the Saturday snapshot
psql -p 6432 pgbouncer -c "SHOW STATS;"
# total_requests | total_received | avg_wait_time
# 2847001 | 2847001 | 847ms847ms average wait at peak. Customers felt that as 12s p99 API latency.
After fix: avg wait 2ms. Same traffic. Same pods. Closed pools. Right math.
Friday deploy postmortem — one line
Missing await pool.close(). One line. 38 minutes Saturday degradation. $2,100 revenue.
Postmortem action items: lifespan lint, pool budget CI, idle_in_transaction_session_timeout, deploy connection graph alert.
Four preventions for one omission. Appropriate ratio.

Connection pool math shows up in Froquiz’s production database scenarios — practice the PgBouncer diagnosis before too many clients already hits your Saturday morning. → Froquiz
